JSC370 2026: Midterm Project

David Jiang

Introduction

The dataset used is the World Health Organization’s (WHO) Global Health Observatory’s (GBO) data. The focus of the data obtained from this dataset will be on HIV data, health personnel data, and economic data, specifically economic data as a whole and for health/medical related uses. The question is whether health/medical spendings and personnel affect the spread of HIV, and if so, by how much. The hypotheses is that these factors do indeed affect the spread of HIV, especially the spendings and personnel counts.

Methods

First, a general list of indicators were queried using WHO’s GBO’s API. Then, the list was inspected and indicators useful for analysing the key question were selected. The data for number of new HIV infections and domestic general government health expenditure as percentage of general government expenditure were obtained directly from WHO’s GBO via the API. However, API queries for other indicators of interest responded successfully (status code 200), but contained no data. As such, the information has been retrieved in the form of CSV files.

The data’s features were then inspected, and all unnecessary columns and columns with no data were dropped, leaving only the country, region, year, and value (e.g. number of new cases of HIV). Note that the regions include but are not the same as the continents. Specifically, the regions include: Easter Mediterranean, Africa, Europe, Americas, Western Pacific, and South-East Asia.

Preliminary Results

To get a rough idea of the amount of missing data, Figure 1 was generated. Based on Figure 1, There are quite a lot of missing values, with there being around 40-50% of the entries being missing for the health personnel. On top of that, there are around 26% of entries missing the new HIV cases value. With this in mind, I then checked the missing values based on year instead, which produced the result in Figure 2. From Figure 2, it can be seen that years 1990-1999 have extremely high proportion of missing, plus the expenditure values for 2024. With this in mind, it was decided that the years 1990-1999 and 2024 would be dropped. Since it is impossible to analyze the years where a country has no data on the number of new cases, entries with no data on new cases were dropped.

After checking the proportion of data still missing, Figures 3, 4, and 5 reveal that the main issue in the high amount of missing data for the features remain. Figure 5 in particular shows the higher number of missing data in Africa.

I then decided to fit several models to check whether any of the features could be dropped due to low significance. The results are shown in Figure 6. We can see that when any of the personnel features are dropped, government health expenses becomes no longer significant at the 0.05 level. However, in the full model, all features appear to be significant at the 0.05 level. Comparing the r-squared, there does not appear to be any significant difference for any of the models. Furthermore, in the last model, the one with only personnel data, both the nurse/midwives and pharmacist features are no longer significant at the 0.05 level.

Based on these values, it is a concern that there could be significant multicollinearity among the features, so VIF was used to compare the features, which are displayed in Figure 7. Based on the results, there is indeed significant multicollinearity, but not severely so, as the values are around 5-7. To reduce the multicollinearity, I then decided to fit a model that only takes in the total personnel within the three categories per 10 000 people. The results, displayed in Figure 8, show that the health expenditure is not statistically significant at the 0.05 level when using the total personnel count. This makes sense, as a sizable sum of a government’s health sector expenditure is likely towards the personnel.

From here, I then decided to impute linearly much of the originally missing values and refit some models, whose results are shown in Figure 9. The imputations are done on a country by country basis, using the most recent existing years with data to impute. In terms of the r-squared, proportionally speaking, removing the doctor count reduces the performance to a more noticable degree than removing other features. Also notably, with the exception of nurse/midwife and pharmacist counts, other features are shown as non-significant in any model when using the imputed data. Even pharmacist count was only statistically insignificant when health expenditure was excluded as a feature. Though there is a decrease in r-squared when compared to the models not using the imputed dataset.

Summary

At this point, I have investigated the missing data to a substantial extent. In terms of the question, from the models fitted, whose details are shown in Figures 6, 8, and 9, the coefficients seem to suggest that more health personnel slows the spread of HIV, while increased spending hastens the spread. However, in the plots without the health expenditure feature, the coefficients are smaller than in the models with it, which is counter intuitive. For future steps, I intend to model both the imputed and non-imputed datasets to compare results and perhaps see if the imputed model is valid. I will use both XGB and linear regression to model, as well as compare the use of 4 models: the all features, nurse count dropped, health expense dropped, and total personnel count. After that analysis has been completed, I will make a conclusion in regards to the question, then develop the website and create make the interactive visualizations.
